content:
# Numbers Chapter 24 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 177–178; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0131–0132 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_024_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_024_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_024_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `NUM_024_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — THE SPIRIT DESCENDS, THE DIVINER BECOMES A PROPHET:** Numbers 24 records the dramatic transformation of Balaam's method and the escalation of his oracles. In the first two oracle cycles (chapter 23), Balaam withdrew to seek omens (נְחָשִׁים, nekhashim); now, in 24:1, he abandons divination altogether and turns his face toward the wilderness where Israel is encamped. The Spirit of God (רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים) falls upon him — language previously reserved for Israel's own charismatic leaders — and his third oracle erupts as visionary poetry of extraordinary power. The chapter contains four distinct oracles: the third (vv. 3–9) and fourth (vv. 15–19) address Israel's present blessing and future dominion, while two shorter oracles (vv. 20–22, 23–24) pronounce doom upon Amalek, the Kenites, Asshur, and Eber. The chapter's literary architecture builds to the messianic climax of v. 17 — "a star shall march forth from Jacob and a scepter shall rise from Israel" — one of the most consequential prophetic utterances in the Torah. In the Leningrad Codex, Numbers 24 continues on page 177 (from where chapter 23 concluded) and extends through page 178. In Codex Vaticanus, the chapter spans pa_0131–0132, with section markers ΝΔ and subsequent numbering visible in the margins.

---

## The Spirit Falls upon Balaam (vv. 1–2)

**1** And-saw [va-yar] Balaam [Bil'am] that [ki] [it was] good [tov] in-the-eyes of [be-einei] YHWH to-bless [le-varekh] [et] Israel [Yisra'el], and-he-did-not [ve-lo] go [halakh] as-time [ke-fa'am] after-time [be-fa'am] to-meet [liqrat] divinations [nekhashim], and-he-set [va-yashet] toward [el] the-wilderness [ha-midbar] his-face [panav].

> **Key terms:** וַיַּרְא בִּלְעָם כִּי טוֹב בְּעֵינֵי יְהוָה לְבָרֵךְ אֶת יִשְׂרָאֵל (va-yar Bil'am ki tov be-einei YHWH le-varekh et Yisra'el) = "and Balaam saw that it was good in the eyes of YHWH to bless Israel" — after two oracle cycles, Balaam has finally understood the divine intention. The verb ראה (ra'ah, "to see") marks a shift from external ritual to internal perception. כְּפַעַם בְּפַעַם (ke-fa'am be-fa'am) = "as time after time" — literally "as stroke after stroke," referring to the previous two occasions when he went to seek omens. נְחָשִׁים (nekhashim) = "divinations/omens" — from נחשׁ (nakhash, "to practice divination, to read omens"), the same root as the noun in 23:23 where Balaam declared there is no divination in Israel. The irony is now complete: the diviner abandons his own craft. וַיָּשֶׁת אֶל הַמִּדְבָּר פָּנָיו (va-yashet el ha-midbar panav) = "and he set toward the wilderness his face" — the verb שׁית (shit, "to set/place") with "face" indicates deliberate orientation. Instead of withdrawing to a solitary height to seek omens, Balaam turns to face the encamped tribes of Israel directly. **LXX:** καὶ ἰδὼν Βαλααμ ὅτι καλόν ἐστιν ἔναντι κυρίου εὐλογεῖν τὸν Ισραηλ, οὐκ ἐπορεύθη κατὰ τὸ εἰωθὸς εἰς συνάντησιν τοῖς οἰωνοῖς ("and Balaam, seeing that it was good before the Lord to bless Israel, did not go as was customary to meet the omens") — the LXX renders נְחָשִׁים as οἰωνοῖς ("bird omens/auguries"), specifying the type of divination.

**2** And-lifted-up [va-yissa] Balaam [Bil'am] [et] his-eyes [einav] and-he-saw [va-yar] [et] Israel [Yisra'el] dwelling [shokhen] according-to-its-tribes [li-shvatav], and-came [va-tehi] upon-him [alav] the-Spirit of [ru'akh] God [Elohim].

> **Key terms:** שֹׁכֵן לִשְׁבָטָיו (shokhen li-shvatav) = "dwelling according to its tribes" — the participle שֹׁכֵן (shokhen, from שׁכן, "to dwell/tabernacle") echoes 23:9 where Israel "dwells alone." Now Balaam sees the tribal encampment spread before him in its ordered arrangement (cf. Numbers 2). The preposition לְ with שְׁבָטָיו indicates distribution: "tribe by tribe." וַתְּהִי עָלָיו רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים (va-tehi alav ru'akh Elohim) = "and the Spirit of God came upon him" — this is the decisive moment of the chapter. The phrase רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים (ru'akh Elohim, "Spirit of God") is the same expression used for the spirit that rushed upon Saul (1 Sam 10:10; 11:6) and the judges (Judg 3:10; 6:34; 11:29). Balaam, the pagan diviner, is now seized by the same empowering presence that animates Israel's own prophetic and charismatic leaders. **LXX:** καὶ ἐξάρας τοὺς ὀφθαλμοὺς αὐτοῦ καθορᾷ τὸν Ισραηλ ἐστρατοπεδευκότα κατὰ φυλάς, καὶ ἐγένετο πνεῦμα θεοῦ ἐν αὐτῷ ("and lifting up his eyes he beheld Israel encamped according to tribes, and the Spirit of God came upon him") — the LXX adds ἐστρατοπεδευκότα ("encamped in military formation"), making explicit the military character of the tribal arrangement.

---

## The Third Oracle: The Vision of the Open Eye (vv. 3–9)

**3** And-he-took-up [va-yissa] his-oracle [meshalo] and-said [va-yomer]: "The-utterance of [ne'um] Balaam [Bil'am] son of [beno] Beor [Be'or], and-the-utterance of [u-ne'um] the-man [ha-gever] whose-eye [shethum] is-opened [ha-ayin]:

> **Key terms:** נְאֻם בִּלְעָם בְּנוֹ בְעֹר (ne'um Bil'am beno Ve'or) = "the utterance of Balaam son of Beor" — the noun נְאֻם (ne'um, "utterance/oracle/declaration") is the technical term for a divine oracle, almost exclusively used in the formula נְאֻם יְהוָה ("utterance of YHWH") in the prophetic books. That Balaam uses it for his own speech indicates the prophetic authority he now claims. וּנְאֻם הַגֶּבֶר שְׁתֻם הָעָיִן (u-ne'um ha-gever shethum ha-ayin) = "and the utterance of the man whose eye is opened" — the word שְׁתֻם (shethum) is a notorious crux. It may derive from שׁתם ("to open") meaning "opened" — the eye of prophetic vision is uncovered; or from שׂתם ("to close/stop up") meaning "closed" — the physical eye is shut while the inner eye sees. The Leningrad Codex vocalizes שְׁתֻם with a shureq, supporting the passive participle meaning. The singular הָעָיִן (ha-ayin, "the eye") rather than the dual is deliberate: it is the singular visionary eye, the prophetic organ of perception. **LXX:** φησὶν Βαλααμ υἱὸς Βεωρ, φησὶν ὁ ἄνθρωπος ὁ ἀληθινῶς ὁρῶν ("says Balaam son of Beor, says the man who truly sees") — the LXX resolves the ambiguity decisively by rendering שְׁתֻם הָעָיִן as ἀληθινῶς ὁρῶν ("truly seeing"), interpreting the opened eye as genuine prophetic vision.

**4** The-utterance of [ne'um] the-one-who-hears [shomea] the-words of [imrei] God [El], who [asher] the-vision of [makhazeh] Shaddai [Shaddai] he-sees [yekhezeh], fallen [nofel] and-uncovered of [u-gelui] eyes [einayim]:

> **Key terms:** נְאֻם שֹׁמֵעַ אִמְרֵי אֵל (ne'um shomea imrei El) = "the utterance of the one who hears the words of God" — Balaam's self-identification escalates: he is not merely a man whose eye is opened but one who hears the very words (אִמְרֵי, imrei, from אֵמֶר, "word/saying") of El. אֲשֶׁר מַחֲזֵה שַׁדַּי יֶחֱזֶה (asher makhazeh Shaddai yekhezeh) = "who the vision of Shaddai he sees" — the noun מַחֲזֶה (makhazeh, "vision") from חזה (khazah, "to see/envision") is a term for prophetic vision, as is the cognate verb יֶחֱזֶה (yekhezeh). The divine name שַׁדַּי (Shaddai, "the Almighty") is the patriarchal name for God (Gen 17:1; Exod 6:3). נֹפֵל וּגְלוּי עֵינָיִם (nofel u-gelui einayim) = "fallen and uncovered of eyes" — the participle נֹפֵל (nofel, "falling/fallen") describes the prophetic trance: Balaam falls prostrate while his eyes are "uncovered" (גָּלוּי, galui, from גלה, "to uncover/reveal"). The physical collapse accompanies the opening of spiritual sight. Now the dual עֵינָיִם (einayim, "eyes") replaces the singular עַיִן of v. 3 — both physical and spiritual sight are engaged. **LXX:** φησὶν ἀκούων λόγια θεοῦ, ὅστις ὅρασιν θεοῦ εἶδεν, ἐν ὕπνῳ, ἀποκεκαλυμμένοι οἱ ὀφθαλμοὶ αὐτοῦ ("says the one hearing the oracles of God, who saw a vision of God in sleep, his eyes having been uncovered") — the LXX adds ἐν ὕπνῳ ("in sleep"), interpreting נֹפֵל as falling asleep — a dream-vision rather than a waking trance. It also renders שַׁדַּי simply as θεοῦ ("of God").

**5** How [mah] are-good [tovu] your-tents [ohalekha], Jacob [Ya'aqov], your-dwelling-places [mishkenotekha], Israel [Yisra'el]!

> **Key terms:** מַה טֹּבוּ אֹהָלֶיךָ יַעֲקֹב מִשְׁכְּנֹתֶיךָ יִשְׂרָאֵל (mah tovu ohalekha Ya'aqov mishkenotekha Yisra'el) = "how good are your tents, O Jacob, your dwelling places, O Israel!" — one of the most celebrated verses in the Hebrew Bible, used in Jewish liturgy as the opening prayer upon entering a synagogue. The exclamatory מַה (mah, "how!") expresses Balaam's overwhelmed response to the sight of the tribal encampment. אֹהָלֶיךָ (ohalekha, "your tents") refers to the physical dwellings of the encamped tribes, while מִשְׁכְּנֹתֶיךָ (mishkenotekha, "your dwelling places") uses the noun מִשְׁכָּן (mishkan, "tabernacle/dwelling"), the same word used for YHWH's own Tabernacle (Exod 25:9). The parallelism tents // dwelling-places, Jacob // Israel follows standard Hebrew poetic structure. **LXX:** ὡς καλοί σου οἱ οἶκοι, Ιακωβ, αἱ σκηναί σου, Ισραηλ ("how beautiful are your houses, O Jacob, your tents, O Israel") — the LXX reverses the order of the parallel terms, placing οἶκοι ("houses") first and σκηναί ("tents") second.

**6** As-valleys [ki-nekhalim] they-are-stretched-out [nittayu], as-gardens [ke-gannot] by [alei] a-river [nahar], as-aloes [ka-ahalim] YHWH has-planted [nata], as-cedars [ka-arazim] by [alei] waters [mayim].

> **Key terms:** כִּנְחָלִים נִטָּיוּ (ki-nekhalim nittayu) = "as valleys/wadis they are stretched out" — the noun נַחַל (nakhal, "wadi/valley stream") refers to the seasonal watercourses of the Near East, lined with vegetation. The niphal of נטה (natah, "to stretch out/extend") describes the sprawling extent of Israel's camp. כְּגַנֹּת עֲלֵי נָהָר (ke-gannot alei nahar) = "as gardens by a river" — the image of irrigated gardens evokes Eden and abundance. כַּאֲהָלִים נָטַע יְהוָה (ka-ahalim nata YHWH) = "as aloes YHWH has planted" — the אֲהָלִים (ahalim, "aloes") are the fragrant aloe trees (Aquilaria agallocha), prized in the ancient world. That YHWH himself has planted them elevates Israel from a human encampment to a divine garden. כַּאֲרָזִים עֲלֵי מָיִם (ka-arazim alei mayim) = "as cedars by waters" — the אֶרֶז (erez, "cedar") is the great cedar of Lebanon, the most majestic tree of the biblical world. Four botanical images — wadis, gardens, aloes, cedars — all associated with water, depict Israel as a place of extravagant, divinely-watered life. **LXX:** ὡσεὶ νάπαι σκιάζουσαι, ὡσεὶ παράδεισοι ἐπὶ ποταμῶν, ὡσεὶ σκηναὶ ἃς ἔπηξεν κύριος, ὡσεὶ κέδροι παρ᾿ ὕδασιν ("as shading glens, as gardens upon rivers, as tents which the Lord pitched, as cedars beside waters") — the LXX renders כַּאֲהָלִים as σκηναί ("tents"), apparently reading אֹהָלִים (ohalim, "tents") instead of אֲהָלִים (ahalim, "aloes") — a significant variant based on the consonantal ambiguity.

**7** Water [mayim] shall-flow [yizzal] from-his-two-buckets [mi-dalyav], and-his-seed [ve-zar'o] [shall be] in-many [be-rabbim] waters [mayim]. And-shall-be-higher [ve-yarom] than-Agag [me-Agag] his-king [malko], and-shall-be-exalted [ve-tinnase] his-kingdom [malkhuto].

> **Key terms:** יִזַּל מַיִם מִדָּלְיָו (yizzal mayim mi-dalyav) = "water shall flow from his two buckets" — the verb זלל (zalal) or נזל (nazal, "to flow/drip") in the qal imperfect, describing abundant overflow. The dual דָּלְיָו (dalyav, "his two buckets") evokes the pastoral image of water drawn in such abundance that it overflows — a metaphor for Israel's prosperity and fertility. וְזַרְעוֹ בְּמַיִם רַבִּים (ve-zar'o be-mayim rabbim) = "and his seed shall be in many waters" — seed (זֶרַע, zera) sown in well-watered land produces an abundant harvest. וְיָרֹם מֵאֲגַג מַלְכּוֹ (ve-yarom me-Agag malko) = "and his king shall be higher than Agag" — Agag is the royal title (or personal name) of the Amalekite kings. The prophecy that Israel's king will surpass Agag finds specific fulfillment in Saul's defeat of Agag (1 Sam 15). The verb רום (rum, "to be high/exalted") in the qal imperfect. וְתִנַּשֵּׂא מַלְכֻתוֹ (ve-tinnase malkhuto) = "and his kingdom shall be exalted" — the hitpael of נשׂא (nasa, "to lift up"), indicating self-exaltation or divinely-granted exaltation of the kingdom. **LXX:** ἐξελεύσεται ἄνθρωπος ἐκ τοῦ σπέρματος αὐτοῦ καὶ κυριεύσει ἐθνῶν πολλῶν, καὶ ὑψωθήσεται ἢ Γωγ βασιλεία αὐτοῦ ("a man shall come forth from his seed and shall rule over many nations, and his kingdom shall be exalted above Gog") — the LXX reads Γωγ (Gog) instead of Agag, a remarkable variant that links this oracle to the eschatological enemy of Ezekiel 38–39. This may reflect an interpretive tradition that identified Agag with the apocalyptic Gog, or it may preserve an alternate Hebrew reading.

**8** God [El] [is] bringing-him-out [motsi'o] from-Egypt [mi-Mitsrayim]; as-the-horns of [ke-to'afot] the-wild-ox [re'em] [are] to-him [lo]. He-shall-devour [yokhal] nations [goyim], his-adversaries [tsarav], and-their-bones [ve-atsmoteihem] he-shall-break [yegarem], and-his-arrows [ve-khitstsav] he-shall-shatter [yimkhats].

> **Key terms:** אֵל מוֹצִיאוֹ מִמִּצְרָיִם (El motsi'o mi-Mitsrayim) = "God is bringing him out from Egypt" — nearly identical to 23:22, but with the singular suffix מוֹצִיאוֹ ("bringing him out") instead of the plural מוֹצִיאָם ("bringing them out"). The shift from plural to singular may reflect the increasingly personalized, even messianic, focus of the oracle. כְּתוֹעֲפֹת רְאֵם לוֹ (ke-to'afot re'em lo) = "as the horns of the wild ox are to him" — repeated from 23:22. יֹאכַל גּוֹיִם צָרָיו (yokhal goyim tsarav) = "he shall devour nations, his adversaries" — the verb אכל (akhal, "to eat/devour") applied to nations depicts total conquest. צָרָיו (tsarav, "his adversaries") from צַר (tsar, "adversary/enemy"). וְעַצְמֹתֵיהֶם יְגָרֵם (ve-atsmoteihem yegarem) = "and their bones he shall break/gnaw" — the verb גרם (garam, "to break bones, to gnaw") is rare and vivid: the lion-like conqueror crushes the very bones of his enemies. וְחִצָּיו יִמְחָץ (ve-khitstsav yimkhats) = "and his arrows he shall shatter" — the verb מחץ (makhats, "to shatter/wound/crush") describes the destructive force of Israel's military strikes. **LXX:** θεὸς ὡδήγησεν αὐτὸν ἐξ Αἰγύπτου· ὡς δόξα μονοκέρωτος αὐτῷ· ἔδεται ἔθνη ἐχθρῶν αὐτοῦ καὶ τὰ πάχη αὐτῶν ἐκμυελιεῖ καὶ ταῖς βολίσιν αὐτοῦ κατατοξεύσει ἐχθρόν ("God led him out of Egypt; as the glory of a unicorn is to him; he shall eat the nations of his enemies and shall extract the marrow of their fat parts and shall shoot down the enemy with his arrows").

**9** He-crouched [kara], he-lay-down [shakhav] as-a-lion [ka-ari], and-as-a-lioness [u-khe-lavi] — who [mi] shall-rouse-him [yeqimennu]? Those-who-bless-you [mevarakhekha] [are] blessed [barukh], and-those-who-curse-you [ve-orerekha] [are] cursed [arur].

> **Key terms:** כָּרַע שָׁכַב כַּאֲרִי וּכְלָבִיא מִי יְקִימֶנּוּ (kara shakhav ka-ari u-khe-lavi mi yeqimennu) = "he crouched, he lay down as a lion and as a lioness — who shall rouse him?" — this couplet echoes Genesis 49:9 (Jacob's blessing of Judah) almost verbatim: "he couched as a lion, and as a lioness — who shall rouse him?" The verb כרע (kara, "to crouch/kneel") and שׁכב (shakhav, "to lie down") describe the resting lion whose rest is itself a threat — no one dares disturb him. מְבָרְכֶיךָ בָרוּךְ וְאֹרְרֶיךָ אָרוּר (mevarakhekha varukh ve-orerekha arur) = "those who bless you are blessed, and those who curse you are cursed" — this formula echoes the Abrahamic covenant of Genesis 12:3 ("I will bless those who bless you and curse the one who curses you") and Genesis 27:29 (Isaac's blessing of Jacob). The oracle's conclusion thus links Israel's present condition to the patriarchal promises: the covenant stands, and the one who curses Israel calls down a curse upon himself. **LXX:** κατακλιθεὶς ἀνεπαύσατο ὡς λέων καὶ ὡς σκύμνος· τίς ἀναστήσει αὐτόν; οἱ εὐλογοῦντές σε εὐλόγηνται, καὶ οἱ καταρώμενοί σε κεκατήρανται ("having lain down he rested as a lion and as a cub — who shall raise him up? Those who bless you are blessed, and those who curse you are cursed").

---

## Balak's Fury and Balaam's Response (vv. 10–14)

**10** And-was-kindled [va-yikhar] the-anger of [af] Balak [Balaq] against [el] Balaam [Bil'am], and-he-struck [va-yisphoq] [et] his-hands [kappav]. And-said [va-yomer] Balak [Balaq] unto [el] Balaam [Bil'am]: "To-curse [laqov] my-enemies [oyevai] I-called-you [qeratikha], and-behold [ve-hinneh] blessing [barekh] you-have-blessed [berakhta] these [zeh] three [shalosh] times [pe'amim]!

> **Key terms:** וַיִּחַר אַף בָּלָק (va-yikhar af Balaq) = "and the anger of Balak was kindled" — the standard Hebrew idiom for intense fury; the "nose" (אַף, af) burns hot with rage. וַיִּסְפֹּק אֶת כַּפָּיו (va-yisphoq et kappav) = "and he struck his hands" — the verb ספק (safaq, "to strike/clap") with כַּפַּיִם (kappayim, "palms/hands") is a gesture of frustration and contempt (cf. Job 27:23; Lam 2:15). בָּרֵךְ בֵּרַכְתָּ זֶה שָׁלֹשׁ פְּעָמִים (barekh berakhta zeh shalosh pe'amim) = "blessing you have blessed these three times" — the infinitive absolute construction emphasizes the completeness of the blessing, and the number שָׁלֹשׁ (shalosh, "three") marks the fullness of Balak's frustration: three attempts, three blessings, zero curses. **LXX:** καὶ ἐθυμώθη Βαλακ ἐπὶ Βαλααμ καὶ συνεκρότησεν ταῖς χερσὶν αὐτοῦ ("and Balak was enraged against Balaam and struck together his hands").

**11** And-now [ve-attah], flee [berakh] for-yourself [lekha] unto [el] your-place [meqomekha]. I-said [amarti] I-shall-surely-honor-you [kabbed akhabeddekha], and-behold [ve-hinneh] YHWH has-withheld-you [mena'akha] from [min] honor [kavod]."

> **Key terms:** בְּרַח לְךָ אֶל מְקוֹמֶךָ (berakh lekha el meqomekha) = "flee to your place" — the imperative ברח (barakh, "to flee") is a dismissal loaded with contempt; Balak does not say "go" (לֵךְ) but "flee" — as if Balaam were in danger. כַּבֵּד אֲכַבֶּדְךָ (kabbed akhabeddekha) = "surely honoring I shall honor you" — the infinitive absolute construction (כַּבֵּד + imperfect) expresses the emphatic promise of honor that Balak made when hiring Balaam (cf. 22:17, 37). מְנָעֲךָ מִן כָּבוֹד (mena'akha min kavod) = "he has withheld you from honor" — the verb מנע (mana, "to withhold/restrain") with מִן (min, "from") — Balak blames YHWH for depriving Balaam of the payment he would have received for a successful curse. **LXX:** νῦν οὖν φεῦγε εἰς τὸν τόπον σου· εἶπα Τιμήσω σε, καὶ νῦν ἐστέρησέν σε κύριος τῆς δόξης ("now therefore flee to your place; I said I would honor you, and now the Lord has deprived you of glory").

**12** And-said [va-yomer] Balaam [Bil'am] unto [el] Balak [Balaq]: "Did-I-not [ha-lo] also [gam] unto [el] your-messengers [malakhekha] whom [asher] you-sent [shalakhta] unto-me [elai] speak [dibbarti] saying [le'mor]:

> **Key terms:** Balaam's defense appeals to his original warning delivered to Balak's first delegation. The noun מַלְאָכֶיךָ (malakhekha, "your messengers") from מַלְאָךְ (mal'akh, "messenger/angel") refers to the envoys of 22:5. The verb שׁלח (shalakh, "to send") in the perfect confirms the historical reference. **LXX:** καὶ εἶπεν Βαλααμ πρὸς Βαλακ Οὐχὶ καὶ τοῖς ἀγγέλοις σου οὓς ἀπέστειλας πρός με ἐλάλησα λέγων ("and Balaam said to Balak, 'Did I not also speak to your messengers whom you sent to me, saying...'").

**13** 'If [im] Balak [Balaq] were-to-give [yitten] to-me [li] the-fullness of [melo] his-house [beito] of-silver [kesef] and-gold [ve-zahav], I-am-not-able [lo ukhal] to-transgress [la'avor] [et] the-mouth of [pi] YHWH to-do [la'asot] good [tovah] or [o] evil [ra'ah] from-my-own [mi-libbi] heart [libbi]; [et] that-which [asher] YHWH shall-speak [yedabber], [et]-that [oto] I-shall-speak [adabber].'

> **Key terms:** מְלֹא בֵיתוֹ כֶּסֶף וְזָהָב (melo veito kesef ve-zahav) = "the fullness of his house of silver and gold" — a hyperbolic expression for unlimited wealth, identical to Balaam's statement in 22:18. לֹא אוּכַל לַעֲבֹר אֶת פִּי יְהוָה (lo ukhal la'avor et pi YHWH) = "I am not able to transgress the mouth of YHWH" — the verb עבר (avar, "to cross over/transgress") with פִּי יְהוָה (pi YHWH, "the mouth of YHWH") means to go beyond or violate the divine command. לַעֲשׂוֹת טוֹבָה אוֹ רָעָה מִלִּבִּי (la'asot tovah o ra'ah mi-libbi) = "to do good or evil from my own heart" — Balaam cannot act independently; his speech is bound to YHWH's word, whether the outcome is favorable or unfavorable. **LXX:** Ἐὰν δῷ μοι Βαλακ πλήρη τὸν οἶκον αὐτοῦ ἀργυρίου καὶ χρυσίου, οὐ δυνήσομαι παραβῆναι τὸ ῥῆμα κυρίου ("if Balak should give me his house full of silver and gold, I shall not be able to transgress the word of the Lord").

**14** And-now [ve-attah] behold-me [hineni] going [holekh] to-my-people [le-ammi]. Come [lekhah], I-shall-counsel-you [i'atsekha] [as to] what [asher] this [ha-zeh] people [ha-am] shall-do [ya'aseh] to-your-people [le-ammekha] in-the-end of [be-akharit] the-days [ha-yamim]."

> **Key terms:** הִנְנִי הוֹלֵךְ לְעַמִּי (hineni holekh le-ammi) = "behold me, going to my people" — the participial construction indicates imminent departure. לְכָה אִיעָצְךָ (lekhah i'atsekha) = "come, I shall counsel you" — the verb יעץ (ya'ats, "to counsel/advise") introduces the final oracle not as a curse but as prophetic intelligence about the future. אֲשֶׁר יַעֲשֶׂה הָעָם הַזֶּה לְעַמְּךָ (asher ya'aseh ha-am ha-zeh le-ammekha) = "what this people shall do to your people" — the reversal is complete: instead of what Balaam will do to Israel, the prophecy concerns what Israel will do to Moab. בְּאַחֲרִית הַיָּמִים (be-akharit ha-yamim) = "in the end of the days" — the phrase אַחֲרִית הַיָּמִים (akharit ha-yamim, "the latter days/the end of days") is a key eschatological marker in the Hebrew Bible (Gen 49:1; Deut 4:30; Isa 2:2; Mic 4:1; Dan 10:14), pointing to a future that extends beyond the immediate horizon. **LXX:** νῦν ἰδοὺ ἐγὼ ἀποτρέχω εἰς τὸν τόπον μου· δεῦρο συμβουλεύσω σοι τί ποιήσει ὁ λαὸς οὗτος τὸν λαόν σου ἐπ᾿ ἐσχάτου τῶν ἡμερῶν ("now behold, I am running away to my place; come, I shall advise you what this people shall do to your people at the last of the days").

---

## The Fourth Oracle: The Star and the Scepter (vv. 15–19)

**15** And-he-took-up [va-yissa] his-oracle [meshalo] and-said [va-yomer]: "The-utterance of [ne'um] Balaam [Bil'am] son of [beno] Beor [Be'or], and-the-utterance of [u-ne'um] the-man [ha-gever] whose-eye [shethum] is-opened [ha-ayin]:

> **Key terms:** The fourth oracle opens with the identical self-identification formula as the third (v. 3). The repetition of the prophetic credentials — ne'um, son of Beor, the open-eyed man — establishes formal continuity while signaling a new and distinct oracle. **LXX:** identical to v. 3.

**16** The-utterance of [ne'um] the-one-who-hears [shomea] the-words of [imrei] God [El], and-the-one-who-knows [ve-yodea] the-knowledge of [da'at] the-Most-High [Elyon]; the-vision of [makhazeh] Shaddai [Shaddai] he-sees [yekhezeh], fallen [nofel] and-uncovered of [u-gelui] eyes [einayim]:

> **Key terms:** This verse expands upon v. 4 with a crucial addition: וְיֹדֵעַ דַּעַת עֶלְיוֹן (ve-yodea da'at Elyon) = "and the one who knows the knowledge of the Most High" — the verb ידע (yada, "to know") with the cognate noun דַּעַת (da'at, "knowledge") and the divine title עֶלְיוֹן (Elyon, "Most High"). The title Elyon appears in the Melchizedek narrative (Gen 14:18–22) and in the Song of Moses (Deut 32:8). Balaam now claims access to the knowledge of the supreme deity — a claim that transcends his pagan credentials. The four divine titles accumulated across vv. 3–4 and 15–16 — El, Shaddai, YHWH (implicit), and now Elyon — represent a comprehensive divine naming that encompasses all dimensions of God's being. **LXX:** φησὶν ἀκούων λόγια θεοῦ, ἐπιστάμενος ἐπιστήμην παρὰ ὑψίστου καὶ ὅρασιν θεοῦ ἰδών, ἐν ὕπνῳ, ἀποκεκαλυμμένοι οἱ ὀφθαλμοὶ αὐτοῦ ("says the one hearing the oracles of God, knowing knowledge from the Most High and having seen a vision of God, in sleep, his eyes having been uncovered") — the LXX renders עֶלְיוֹן as ὑψίστου ("Most High"), the standard Greek equivalent.

**17** I-see-him [er'ennu] but-not [ve-lo] now [attah]; I-behold-him [ashurenu] but-not [ve-lo] near [qarov]. A-star [kokhav] shall-march-forth [darakh] from-Jacob [mi-Ya'aqov], and-a-scepter [ve-shevet] shall-rise [ve-qam] from-Israel [mi-Yisra'el], and-he-shall-crush [u-makhats] the-corners of [pa'atei] Moab [Mo'av], and-he-shall-shatter [ve-qarqar] all [kol] the-sons of [benei] Seth [Sheth].

> **Key terms:** אֶרְאֶנּוּ וְלֹא עַתָּה אֲשׁוּרֶנּוּ וְלֹא קָרוֹב (er'ennu ve-lo attah ashurenu ve-lo qarov) = "I see him but not now, I behold him but not near" — the two verbs of vision — ראה (ra'ah) and שׁור (shur, "to gaze/behold") — describe prophetic sight into the distant future. The negations "not now" and "not near" establish the temporal distance of the fulfillment. דָּרַךְ כּוֹכָב מִיַּעֲקֹב (darakh kokhav mi-Ya'aqov) = "a star has marched forth from Jacob" — the verb דרך (darakh, "to tread/march/step") is used of treading a path or bending a bow; applied to a star, it suggests a star that strides across the sky with purposeful, conquering motion. The כּוֹכָב (kokhav, "star") became the paramount messianic symbol in later Jewish interpretation: Rabbi Akiva applied it to Bar Kokhba ("son of the star") during the second Jewish revolt (132–135 CE). וְקָם שֵׁבֶט מִיִּשְׂרָאֵל (ve-qam shevet mi-Yisra'el) = "and a scepter shall rise from Israel" — the שֵׁבֶט (shevet) means both "rod/staff" and "scepter," the instrument of royal authority (cf. Gen 49:10, "the scepter shall not depart from Judah"). וּמָחַץ פַּאֲתֵי מוֹאָב (u-makhats pa'atei Mo'av) = "and he shall crush the corners/sides of Moab" — the verb מחץ (makhats, "to crush/shatter") and the noun פֵּאָה (pe'ah, "corner/side/temple of the head") — the image may be of crushing the skulls/temples of Moab's warriors or shattering Moab's territorial borders. וְקַרְקַר כָּל בְּנֵי שֵׁת (ve-qarqar kol benei Sheth) = "and he shall shatter all the sons of Seth" — the verb קרקר (qarqar, "to tear down/demolish") is an intensive (pilpel) form of קור/קרר. The "sons of Seth" (בְּנֵי שֵׁת) may refer to all humanity (as descendants of Seth, son of Adam) or, more likely, to the Moabites specifically (שֵׁת being a Moabite clan name or equivalent to שְׁאוֹן, "tumult"). **LXX:** δείξω αὐτῷ, καὶ οὐχὶ νῦν· μακαρίζω, καὶ οὐκ ἐγγίζει. ἀνατελεῖ ἄστρον ἐξ Ιακωβ, καὶ ἀναστήσεται ἄνθρωπος ἐξ Ισραηλ καὶ θραύσει τοὺς ἀρχηγοὺς Μωαβ καὶ προνομεύσει πάντας υἱοὺς Σηθ ("I shall show him, and not now; I bless, and he does not draw near. A star shall rise out of Jacob, and a man shall stand up out of Israel and shall crush the chiefs of Moab and shall plunder all the sons of Seth") — the LXX renders שֵׁבֶט ("scepter") as ἄνθρωπος ("man"), making the messianic figure explicitly personal rather than symbolic.

**18** And-shall-be [ve-hayah] Edom [Edom] a-possession [yereshash], and-shall-be [ve-hayah] a-possession [yereshash] Seir [Se'ir], his-enemies [oyevav]; and-Israel [ve-Yisra'el] shall-do [oseh] valiantly [khayil].

> **Key terms:** וְהָיָה אֱדוֹם יְרֵשָׁה (ve-hayah Edom yereshah) = "and Edom shall be a possession" — the noun יְרֵשָׁה (yereshah, "possession/inheritance") from ירשׁ (yarash, "to possess/dispossess/inherit") indicates that Edom will become conquered territory. וְהָיָה יְרֵשָׁה שֵׂעִיר (ve-hayah yereshah Se'ir) = "and Seir shall be a possession" — Seir is the mountainous region synonymous with Edom (Gen 32:3; 36:8). The repetition of יְרֵשָׁה for both names reinforces the totality of the dispossession. אֹיְבָיו (oyevav, "his enemies") — the suffix refers to Israel: Edom/Seir are Israel's enemies and will become Israel's possession. וְיִשְׂרָאֵל עֹשֶׂה חָיִל (ve-Yisra'el oseh khayil) = "and Israel shall do valiantly" — the expression עשׂה חַיִל (asah khayil, "to do valiantly/show strength") is a military idiom (cf. Ps 60:14; 108:14; 118:15–16). **LXX:** καὶ ἔσται Εδωμ κληρονομία, καὶ ἔσται κληρονομία Ησαυ ὁ ἐχθρὸς αὐτοῦ, καὶ Ισραηλ ἐποίησεν ἐν ἰσχύι ("and Edom shall be an inheritance, and Esau his enemy shall be an inheritance, and Israel acted in strength") — the LXX adds Ησαυ ("Esau"), making explicit the identification of Seir with Esau/Edom.

**19** And-he-shall-rule [ve-yerd] from-Jacob [mi-Ya'aqov], and-he-shall-destroy [ve-he'evid] the-survivor [sarid] from-a-city [me-ir]."

> **Key terms:** וְיֵרְדְּ מִיַּעֲקֹב (ve-yerd mi-Ya'aqov) = "and he shall rule from Jacob" — the verb ירד (yarad) in the basic sense means "to descend," but here in context it carries the sense of רדה (radah, "to have dominion/rule"), a well-attested interchange in poetic texts (cf. Ps 72:8; Judg 5:13). The ruler who emerges from Jacob will exercise dominion. וְהֶאֱבִיד שָׂרִיד מֵעִיר (ve-he'evid sarid me-ir) = "and he shall destroy the survivor from a city" — the hiphil of אבד (avad, "to perish"), meaning "to cause to perish/destroy." The שָׂרִיד (sarid, "survivor/remnant") is the one who escapes battle, and even this fugitive will not survive. The singular עִיר (ir, "city") may refer specifically to the capital of the enemy or generically to any fortified city. **LXX:** καὶ ἐξεγερθήσεται ἐξ Ιακωβ καὶ ἀπολεῖ σῳζόμενον ἐκ πόλεως ("and one shall be raised from Jacob and shall destroy the one saved from a city").

---

## The Short Oracles against the Nations (vv. 20–24)

**20** And-he-saw [va-yar] [et] Amalek [Amaleq], and-he-took-up [va-yissa] his-oracle [meshalo] and-said [va-yomer]: "The-beginning of [reshit] the-nations [goyim] [is] Amalek [Amaleq], and-his-end [ve-akharito] [is] unto-destruction [adei oved]."

> **Key terms:** וַיַּרְא אֶת עֲמָלֵק (va-yar et Amaleq) = "and he saw Amalek" — from the heights of Peor, Balaam now turns his prophetic gaze upon the surrounding nations. רֵאשִׁית גּוֹיִם עֲמָלֵק (reshit goyim Amaleq) = "the beginning of the nations is Amalek" — the noun רֵאשִׁית (reshit, "beginning/firstfruits/chief") may mean that Amalek was the first nation to attack Israel (Exod 17:8–16) or the foremost/mightiest among the nations encountered. וְאַחֲרִיתוֹ עֲדֵי אֹבֵד (ve-akharito adei oved) = "and his end is unto destruction" — the contrast between רֵאשִׁית (reshit, "beginning") and אַחֲרִית (akharit, "end") creates a stark trajectory: however great Amalek's beginning, his final destiny is אֹבֵד (oved, "perishing/destruction"), from אבד (avad, "to perish"). **LXX:** καὶ ἰδὼν τὸν Αμαληκ καὶ ἀνέλαβεν τὴν παραβολὴν αὐτοῦ καὶ εἶπεν Ἀρχὴ ἐθνῶν Αμαληκ, καὶ τὸ σπέρμα αὐτῶν ἀπολεῖται ("and seeing Amalek he took up his oracle and said: 'The beginning of nations is Amalek, and their seed shall perish'") — the LXX renders אַחֲרִיתוֹ ("his end") as τὸ σπέρμα αὐτῶν ("their seed"), shifting from temporal destiny to genealogical extinction.

**21** And-he-saw [va-yar] [et] the-Kenite [ha-Qeini], and-he-took-up [va-yissa] his-oracle [meshalo] and-said [va-yomer]: "Enduring [eitan] [is] your-dwelling-place [moshavekha], and-set [ve-sim] in-the-rock [ba-sela] your-nest [qinnekha].

> **Key terms:** וַיַּרְא אֶת הַקֵּינִי (va-yar et ha-Qeini) = "and he saw the Kenite" — the Kenites were a semi-nomadic people associated with metalworking, connected to Moses through his father-in-law Jethro/Hobab (Judg 1:16; 4:11). אֵיתָן מוֹשָׁבֶךָ (eitan moshavekha) = "enduring is your dwelling place" — the adjective אֵיתָן (eitan, "enduring/permanent/perennial") describes strength and permanence. The wordplay is central: וְשִׂים בַּסֶּלַע קִנֶּךָ (ve-sim ba-sela qinnekha) = "and set in the rock is your nest" — the noun קֵן (qen, "nest") creates a pun on קֵינִי (Qeini, "Kenite"), and the name קַיִן (Qayin, "Cain") itself. The Kenite has nested like a bird in the rock — seemingly impregnable. **LXX:** καὶ ἰδὼν τὸν Κεναῖον καὶ ἀναλαβὼν τὴν παραβολὴν αὐτοῦ εἶπεν Ἰσχυρὰ ἡ κατοικία σου· καὶ ἐὰν θῇς ἐν πέτρᾳ τὴν νοσσιάν σου ("and seeing the Kenite and taking up his oracle he said: 'Strong is your dwelling; and if you set in the rock your nest'").

**22** But [ki im] it-shall-be [yihyeh] for-burning [le-va'er] Cain [Qayin]; until-when [ad mah] shall-Asshur [Ashur] take-you-captive [tishbekka]?"

> **Key terms:** כִּי אִם יִהְיֶה לְבַעֵר קָיִן (ki im yihyeh le-va'er Qayin) = "but it shall be for burning, Cain/the Kenite" — despite the seemingly impregnable rock-nest, the Kenite will be consumed. The verb בער (ba'ar, "to burn/consume") in the piel infinitive construct suggests destruction by fire or complete consumption. קָיִן (Qayin) is used here as a collective name for the Kenites. עַד מָה אַשּׁוּר תִּשְׁבֶּךָּ (ad mah Ashur tishbekka) = "until when shall Asshur take you captive?" — the verb שׁבה (shavah, "to take captive") and the name אַשּׁוּר (Ashur, "Assyria") point to the Assyrian deportations that would eventually engulf the region. The rhetorical question "until when?" (עַד מָה) implies that the captivity is already foreseen and its duration uncertain but real. **LXX:** καὶ ἐὰν γένηται τῷ Βεωρ νοσσιὰ πανουργίας, Ασσύριοι αἰχμαλωτεύσουσίν σε ("and if there should be for Beor a nest of craftiness, the Assyrians shall take you captive") — the LXX appears to read בְּעֹר (Be'or, "Beor") for בַּעֵר ("burning"), creating a puzzling reference to Balaam's father.

**23** And-he-took-up [va-yissa] his-oracle [meshalo] and-said [va-yomer]: "Alas [oi], who [mi] shall-live [yikhyeh] when-God-appoints [mi-sumo] this [el]?

> **Key terms:** אוֹי מִי יִחְיֶה מִשֻּׂמוֹ אֵל (oi mi yikhyeh mi-sumo El) = "alas, who shall live when God appoints this?" — the exclamation אוֹי (oi, "alas/woe") introduces a lament of cosmic scope. The question מִי יִחְיֶה (mi yikhyeh, "who shall live?") is existential: when God sets these events in motion, survival itself is in question. The phrase מִשֻּׂמוֹ אֵל (mi-sumo El) is difficult: it may be parsed as "from God's appointing this" (the infinitive construct of שׂים with the divine name), meaning "when God establishes/ordains these things." The verse functions as a transition between the Kenite oracle and the final oracle about ships. **LXX:** καὶ ἰδὼν τὸν Ωγ καὶ ἀναλαβὼν τὴν παραβολὴν αὐτοῦ εἶπεν Ὦ ὦ, τίς ζήσεται ὅταν θῇ ταῦτα ὁ θεός; ("and seeing Og, he took up his oracle and said: 'Oh, oh, who shall live when God shall ordain these things?'") — the LXX reads Ωγ (Og) for an otherwise unclear referent, apparently identifying a specific people as the target of this oracle.

**24** And-ships [ve-tsim] from-the-coast of [mi-yad] Kittim [Kittim], and-they-shall-afflict [ve-innu] Asshur [Ashur], and-they-shall-afflict [ve-innu] Eber [Ever], and-he-also [ve-gam hu] unto-destruction [adei oved]."

> **Key terms:** וְצִים מִיַּד כִּתִּים (ve-tsim mi-yad Kittim) = "and ships from the coast of Kittim" — the noun צִי (tsi, "ship/vessel") in the plural, and כִּתִּים (Kittim) originally referred to Cyprus (from the city of Kition) but later became a general term for the western Mediterranean lands and their naval powers (cf. Dan 11:30; 1 Macc 1:1). מִיַּד (mi-yad) = "from the hand/coast of" — the noun יָד (yad, "hand") can mean "coast/shore" in geographical usage. וְעִנּוּ אַשּׁוּר (ve-innu Ashur) = "and they shall afflict Asshur" — the piel of ענה (anah, "to afflict/humble/oppress"). וְעִנּוּ עֵבֶר (ve-innu Ever) = "and they shall afflict Eber" — עֵבֶר (Ever, "Eber") is the ancestor of the Hebrews (Gen 10:21; 11:14–17); here the name may function as a geographic or ethnic designation for the trans-Euphrates region or the Hebrews themselves. וְגַם הוּא עֲדֵי אֹבֵד (ve-gam hu adei oved) = "and he also unto destruction" — the same phrase used of Amalek in v. 20 (עֲדֵי אֹבֵד), indicating that even the western naval power (Kittim) will ultimately perish. **LXX:** καὶ ἐξελεύσεται ἐκ χειρὸς Κιτιαίων καὶ κακώσουσιν Ασσουρ καὶ κακώσουσιν Εβραίους, καὶ αὐτοὶ ὁμοθυμαδὸν ἀπολοῦνται ("and he shall come forth from the hand of the Kitians and shall afflict Assur and shall afflict the Hebrews, and they together shall perish") — the LXX renders עֵבֶר as Εβραίους ("Hebrews"), making the ethnic identification explicit, and renders the final clause as ὁμοθυμαδὸν ἀπολοῦνται ("they shall perish together"), implying mutual destruction.

---

## Departure (v. 25)

**25** And-rose-up [va-yaqom] Balaam [Bil'am] and-went [va-yelekh] and-returned [va-yashov] to-his-place [li-meqomo]; and-also [ve-gam] Balak [Balaq] went [halakh] on-his-way [le-darko].

> **Key terms:** וַיָּקָם בִּלְעָם וַיֵּלֶךְ וַיָּשָׁב לִמְקוֹמוֹ (va-yaqom Bil'am va-yelekh va-yashov li-meqomo) = "and Balaam rose and went and returned to his place" — three consecutive wayyiqtol verbs create a rapid, matter-of-fact conclusion to the Balaam cycle. The verb שׁוּב (shuv, "to return"), which has served as the chapter's theological keyword since 23:5, now functions in its most literal sense: physical return. Balaam goes back to where he came from — Pethor on the Euphrates (22:5). וְגַם בָּלָק הָלַךְ לְדַרְכּוֹ (ve-gam Balaq halakh le-darko) = "and also Balak went on his way" — the king of Moab departs, his enterprise a total failure. The brevity of the verse — no speeches, no farewell, no further negotiation — conveys the finality of the separation. Yet this is not the end of Balaam's story: Numbers 31:8 reports his death in battle, and 31:16 reveals that he subsequently counseled Moab to seduce Israel through the women of Midian (the Baal-Peor incident of chapter 25). **LXX:** καὶ ἀναστὰς Βαλααμ ἀπῆλθεν ἀποστραφεὶς εἰς τὸν τόπον αὐτοῦ, καὶ Βαλακ ἀπῆλθεν πρὸς ἑαυτόν ("and Balaam arose and departed, having turned back to his place, and Balak departed to himself").

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations from the Leningrad Codex (pp. 177–178)

1. **Chapter location and column layout:** Numbers 24 begins on page 177 of the Leningrad Codex, continuing directly from the conclusion of chapter 23 (which ends with the sacrificial preparation at Peor in 23:30). The text flows through the remainder of page 177 and continues onto page 178. The standard three-column layout is maintained throughout, with Masoretic marginal apparatus (Masorah parva) visible between columns and the Masorah magna in the upper and lower margins.

2. **Parashah divisions within chapter 24:** The Leningrad Codex marks a setumah (ס, closed paragraph) before v. 1, separating the narrative transition from the previous sacrificial scene. The oracle sections (vv. 3–9 and 15–24) are written in continuous prose format, not in the stichometric layout sometimes reserved for poetry. Additional setumah markers appear after v. 9 (end of third oracle), after v. 14 (before the fourth oracle), and after v. 19 (before the short oracles against the nations). These divisions correspond precisely to the literary structure of the oracles.

3. **The word שְׁתֻם (shethum) in vv. 3 and 15:** The Leningrad Codex's vocalization of שְׁתֻם with a shureq (u-class vowel) in the passive participle pattern supports the reading "opened" rather than "closed." No ketiv/qere notation appears in the margin for this word, indicating the Masoretes accepted the received consonantal text and this vocalization without reservation. This is significant given the long history of debate over the meaning of the word.

4. **The divine names in vv. 3–4 and 15–16:** The scribe preserves a careful escalation of divine titles: אֵל (El, vv. 4, 16), שַׁדַּי (Shaddai, vv. 4, 16), and עֶלְיוֹן (Elyon, v. 16 only). The addition of Elyon in the fourth oracle's prologue (v. 16) but not in the third oracle's (v. 4) is consistently maintained with no marginal corrections, confirming this is the received textual tradition, not a scribal error.

5. **The Star Oracle (v. 17):** The consonantal text of v. 17 — דרך כוכב מיעקב וקם שׁבט מישׂראל — is written without any special formatting or ornamentation in the Leningrad Codex, despite the verse's enormous theological significance in later Jewish and Christian interpretation. No Masoretic marginal annotation draws special attention to the messianic content. The vocalization of דָּרַךְ (darakh) as a qal perfect ("has marched forth") rather than an imperfect allows for both completed and prophetic-perfect readings.

6. **The rare verb קרקר (qarqar) in v. 17:** The pilpel form וְקַרְקַר (ve-qarqar, "and he shall shatter/demolish") is one of the rarest verbal forms in the Hebrew Bible. The Leningrad Codex preserves the geminated form clearly, with no evidence of scribal hesitation or correction. The Masorah parva does not flag this form, suggesting the Masoretes regarded it as stable.

### Observations from Codex Vaticanus (pa_0131–0132)

7. **Section markers:** The Vaticanus manuscript shows section numbering in the margins of pa_0131–0132. The transition from chapter 23 to chapter 24 falls within the continuous text on pa_0131. The scriptio continua format continues without interruption, and the oracle sections are not visually distinguished from narrative prose.

8. **LXX "Gog" for MT "Agag" in v. 7:** The most striking textual divergence in the chapter occurs at v. 7, where the Vaticanus reads Γωγ (Gog) where the MT has אֲגַג (Agag). This variant is theologically consequential: it connects Balaam's oracle to the apocalyptic Gog of Ezekiel 38–39, transforming a historical reference (the Amalekite king Agag defeated by Saul) into an eschatological one. The variant may reflect an interpretive tradition that saw in Balaam's oracle a prophecy extending beyond the immediate historical horizon, or it may preserve an alternate Hebrew Vorlage.

9. **LXX "man" for MT "scepter" in v. 17:** The Vaticanus renders שֵׁבֶט (shevet, "scepter") as ἄνθρωπος (anthropos, "man"), personalizing the messianic symbol. Where the MT offers an emblem of royal authority (the scepter), the LXX presents a human figure — a ruler, not merely a symbol of rule. This reading influenced early Christian messianic interpretation and stands alongside the rendering of כּוֹכָב (kokhav, "star") as ἄστρον (astron, "star"), which the LXX preserves literally.

10. **LXX "Og" for an uncertain referent in v. 23:** The Vaticanus reads Ωγ (Og) at v. 23 where the MT has an ambiguous construction (אוֹי מִי יִחְיֶה מִשֻּׂמוֹ אֵל). The LXX appears to have read a proper noun in the text — perhaps parsing מִשֻּׂמוֹ differently — and identified the target of this oracle as Og, king of Bashan (Num 21:33–35; Deut 3:1–11). This reading is not supported by the MT but reflects the LXX translator's attempt to give the short oracles concrete referents.

11. **LXX "Hebrews" for MT "Eber" in v. 24:** The Vaticanus renders עֵבֶר (Ever, "Eber") as Εβραίους (Ebraious, "Hebrews"), making explicit the ethnic identification that the MT leaves ambiguous. This interpretive rendering reflects the standard LXX practice of connecting the ancestor Eber with the later ethnic designation "Hebrew," but it creates a theological difficulty: the oracle appears to predict affliction not only upon Assyria but upon the Hebrews themselves, a reading that the MT's use of the ancestral name "Eber" does not necessarily require.

### Theological and Structural Notes

12. **The transformation of the diviner (v. 1):** The opening verse of chapter 24 marks the decisive turning point in the Balaam narrative. In chapters 22–23, Balaam followed the protocol of a professional diviner: he withdrew to seek omens (נְחָשִׁים), waited for divine encounters, and delivered what he received. In 24:1, he abandons divination altogether. The narrator's comment — "he did not go as time after time to meet divinations" — signals a methodological rupture. From this point forward, Balaam does not seek God through technique; the Spirit of God seizes him directly. The pagan seer becomes, for these oracles, a genuine prophet — though his subsequent counsel (31:16) reveals that the transformation was temporary.

13. **The four-oracle structure and escalation:** The four oracles of chapters 23–24 follow a clear pattern of escalation. The first oracle (23:7–10) declares Israel uncursable and innumerable. The second (23:18–24) declares God immutable and Israel invincible. The third (24:3–9) describes Israel's present beauty and power in lush imagery. The fourth (24:15–19) projects Israel's future into eschatological time with the star-and-scepter prophecy. Each oracle sees farther, says more, and reaches deeper into the purposes of God. The short oracles (vv. 20–24) then sweep outward from Israel to the surrounding nations, declaring the doom of all who oppose the divine plan.

14. **The messianic star and scepter (v. 17):** Verse 17 is among the most consequential prophetic utterances in the Hebrew Bible. The "star from Jacob" and "scepter from Israel" have generated a vast interpretive tradition. In its immediate context, the oracle predicts a future Israelite king who will crush Moab and subjugate the surrounding nations — a prediction fulfilled in part by David's conquests (2 Sam 8:2, 13–14). In later Jewish interpretation, the verse became the paramount messianic proof-text: the Targum Onkelos renders it "a king shall arise from Jacob and a messiah shall be anointed from Israel." Rabbi Akiva applied it to Simon bar Kosiba, renaming him Bar Kokhba ("son of the star"). In Christian interpretation, the star was identified with the star of Bethlehem (Matt 2:2) and the scepter with Christ's universal rule. The Qumran community (CD 7:18–20; 4QTestimonia) cited this verse as a key messianic text. The verse's power lies in its combination of cosmic imagery (star) with political imagery (scepter), suggesting a ruler whose authority is both heavenly and earthly.

15. **The "end of days" and prophetic horizon (v. 14):** Balaam's use of the phrase בְּאַחֲרִית הַיָּמִים (be-akharit ha-yamim, "in the end of the days") in v. 14 places the fourth oracle in an explicitly eschatological frame. This is the same phrase used by Jacob before his final blessings (Gen 49:1) and by Moses in his farewell speech (Deut 4:30; 31:29). It does not necessarily mean "the end of history" in an apocalyptic sense but rather "the latter period" — the time when the long-range consequences of present realities will become manifest. The fourth oracle, introduced by this phrase, thus claims to reveal not merely what will happen next but what will happen when the full arc of divine purpose reaches its culmination. That such a prophecy comes from the mouth of a pagan diviner — against his employer's wishes, at the instigation of YHWH's own Spirit — is one of the most profound ironies in the Torah.
